thread.title: EU Stands Firm: Boosting Military Aid and Support for Ukraine Amid Ongoing Conflict
thread.title_full: EU Stands Firm: Boosting Military Aid and Support for Ukraine Amid Ongoing Conflict

author: newsdirectory3
title: EU Stands Firm: Boosting Military Aid and Support for Ukraine Amid Ongoing Conflict
text:
HRVP Blog: EU Support for Ukraine
I recently visited Kyiv to reaffirm EU support for Ukraine. Despite ongoing Russian attacks, I saw a determined country requesting more arms, ammunition, and funding for its defense.
This was my fifth visit to Ukraine since the invasion began in February 2022. I traveled from Budapest to Kyiv directly, just before the first meeting of EU Ministers for Foreign Affairs and Defence after the US elections. My mission was clear: to reassure Ukraine of our support and understand its defense needs against Russia.
The resilience of the Ukrainian people truly impressed me. I visited a training center and met soldiers on the frontlines. They are not considering defeat; they are united in their fight for their nation. They are aware of the atrocities committed by Russian troops in spring 2022 and continue to witness similar actions in occupied areas.
Yahidne’s Lesson of Justice
In Yahidne, I saw the remnants of a crime against humanity. In a former school, 360 Ukrainians were held by Russian forces for 27 days in cramped conditions. Eleven people died during this ordeal. This situation highlights the need for justice. Ukraine has documented 140,000 war crime cases, and the EU is assisting in these efforts through the EU Advisory Mission.
The people of Yahidne want to ensure that Russians never return. This determination is palpable across the country.
Ukraine’s Call for Strong Support
Ukraine currently faces intense military pressure, particularly in the east. Russian forces have increased attacks on essential infrastructure, trying to plunge Ukraine into darkness and despair. Civilians endure terror from drone and missile assaults almost nightly. While some areas, like Kyiv, are protected by modern air defense, many regions lack such defenses. Even during my visit, missiles struck President Zelenskyy’s hometown of Kryviy Rih.
None of my contacts in Ukraine discussed the possibility of a ceasefire. They believe it would only provide Russia a chance to regroup for further attacks. Ukrainian demands focus on military support to strengthen their position rather than facilitation of peace talks.
Boosting Military Assistance
The EU has provided significant military aid to Ukraine, totaling €45 billion. This represents a 60% increase since my last visit in February 2024. We have fulfilled our promise of supplying 1 million rounds of ammunition and trained 75,000 Ukrainian soldiers. Yet, this assistance is not enough to gain an advantage over Russia, which is fully committed to its war effort.
We need to increase our support and drop restrictive policies limiting aid. Ukraine requires more air defense systems, ammunition, and long-range weapons. Timely support can save lives.
Compared to their contribution, European nations can do more. Our aid amounts to less than €2 per European citizen weekly. Investing in Ukraine’s freedom is a minor cost compared to the potential consequences of a Russian victory, which could cost Europe significantly more.
Investing in Ukraine’s Defense Industry
Investing in Ukraine’s domestic defense capabilities is another vital strategy. Ukraine has developed a strong defense sector producing effective military equipment. I met engineers creating long-range drones, showcasing the innovation and determination within Ukraine.
The EU has allocated €400 million from frozen Russian assets to Ukrainian defense companies. Since 2022, Ukraine’s defense industry has expanded significantly, and there is room for further development. My discussions revealed a desire for a larger share of these funds—ideally €1 billion—for their defense industry.
Considering repurposing frozen Russian assets for Ukraine’s defense is also an option. This action would ensure that Russia’s aggression has significant consequences.
Conclusion
I shared these insights at my final Foreign Affairs Council meeting. Regardless of the situation in the US, Europe must continue supporting Ukraine. This requires additional resources, which can be facilitated through EU debt and new funding sources.
With political unity and commitment, Europe can ensure its own security while supporting Ukraine. As we face these challenges, EU leadership must prioritize this matter for the sake of Ukraine and the future of Europe.
